skill.name: baoyu-url-to-markdown
skill.description: Fetch a web page through Chrome DevTools Protocol and save a cleaned Markdown copy with page metadata.
skill.summary: Convert rendered web pages into Markdown using Chrome CDP.
skill.author: jimliu

security_audit.summary: Static findings for command execution, networking, filesystem writes, and environment access are mostly true positives in a legitimate Chrome CDP capture workflow. I found no evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration, but the skill launches a local browser with a debugging port and can capture authenticated page content, so it should not be published without stronger safeguards and user warnings.

high_findings.title: Chrome Debug Port and Browser Launch
high_findings.description: The skill starts Chrome with a remote debugging port and connects over WebSocket. This is expected for CDP capture, but it creates a powerful local automation channel while the browser session is active.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly opens a loopback TCP port, launches Chrome with remote debugging enabled, and attaches to a page target. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the security impact is clear.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Page Capture Risk
high_findings.description: The documented wait mode supports pages that require login and then captures page HTML into Markdown. This can save private or paid content if the user points it at sensitive pages.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes login-required pages, and the implementation waits for user readiness before extracting rendered HTML. I did not find evidence of network exfiltration beyond the requested page load.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Output Path Writes
medium_findings.description: The command accepts an output path and writes the generated Markdown to that location. This is useful, but a mistaken or hostile invocation could overwrite files the current user can write.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The output path is taken from CLI arguments and passed to writeFile after creating the parent directory. This is standard CLI behavior but still a real filesystem write risk.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Controlled Chrome and Data Paths
medium_findings.description: The skill reads environment variables for the Chrome binary, output data directory, and Chrome profile directory. These controls are documented, but they influence executable and filesystem locations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variables are clearly documented and not hidden credential reads. The risk remains because URL_CHROME_PATH controls the executable that spawn runs.

medium_findings.title: Page-Context Script Evaluation
medium_findings.description: The skill evaluates a bundled extraction script in the loaded page through Runtime.evaluate. The script is static and extraction-focused, but it runs inside arbitrary pages visited by the browser.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evaluated expression comes from the local helper script rather than direct user input. It still has access to the target page DOM and is part of a high-privilege browser automation path.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Backtick False Positives
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown code fences or JavaScript template literals used for strings, timeouts, paths, and Markdown formatting. I did not find evidence that these lines execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples or TypeScript template literals. Actual process creation is limited to child_process.spawn in scripts/cdp.ts.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Regex Literals
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to match regex syntax and words inside descriptions, not cryptographic operations. No hashing, encryption, or digest API use was found in the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant code decodes HTML entities and escapes regex metacharacters. The scanner labels are not supported by semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.spawn of Browser Executable
dangerous_patterns.description: Chrome is launched through child_process.spawn, with URL_CHROME_PATH optionally selecting the executable path. This is a controlled design choice but remains a dangerous pattern for marketplace distribution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import and launch path are explicit. The command arguments are structured arrays, reducing injection risk, but the executable path can be environment-controlled.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local CDP Network Control Channel
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allocates a loopback port, polls the Chrome debugging endpoint, and connects to its WebSocket debugger. This is necessary for CDP but exposes broad browser control while active.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly creates the port and connects to Chrome DevTools over loopback. The port is bound to 127.0.0.1, which limits remote exposure.

content.value_statement: Saving rendered web pages as clean Markdown is difficult when pages use JavaScript or require login. This skill opens Chrome through CDP, extracts readable content, and writes a Markdown document with metadata.
actual_capabilities: Launches Chrome and captures rendered page content through the Chrome DevTools Protocol.
Supports automatic capture after page load and a wait mode for manual login or page preparation.
Converts common HTML elements such as headings, links, images, lists, tables, quotes, and code blocks.
Adds YAML front matter with URL, title, description, author, publication date, and capture time when available.
Writes Markdown files to an automatic domain-based path or a user-specified output path.
limitations: Requires Chrome or a compatible browser executable on the local machine.
Markdown conversion uses regex-based HTML handling, so complex layouts may lose structure.
Captured content may include private information from authenticated sessions.
It does not verify copyright, paywall, or terms-of-service restrictions for captured pages.

use_cases.title: Archive Research Articles
use_cases.description: Save public web articles as Markdown notes with useful metadata for later review.

use_cases.title: Document Product References
use_cases.description: Capture rendered documentation pages or release notes into a local Markdown knowledge base.

use_cases.title: Prepare Source Material
use_cases.description: Convert web pages into editable Markdown before summarizing, annotating, or reorganizing content.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Public Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use baoyu-url-to-markdown to capture this public URL as Markdown: https://example.com/article

prompt_templates.title: Save to a Chosen File
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert https://example.com/docs/page to Markdown and save it as notes/example-docs.md.

prompt_templates.title: Wait for Login
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL with wait mode, let me finish login, then capture the page to Markdown after I confirm it is ready.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Research Folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Capture these three URLs as Markdown, keep each file under url-to-markdown by domain, and report the saved paths.

output_examples.output: A Markdown file containing YAML metadata, the article title, headings, paragraphs, links, and image references.

output_examples.input: A documentation page with tables and code examples
output_examples.output: A Markdown document with preserved headings, lists, tables, block quotes, and fenced code sections where possible.

output_examples.input: A page that requires manual login
output_examples.output: After user confirmation, a local Markdown copy of the visible rendered page content.

best_practices: Use auto mode for public pages and wait mode only when manual interaction is required.
Review captured Markdown before sharing it, especially after using an authenticated browser session.
Set a dedicated Chrome profile directory for this skill to reduce exposure of personal browsing data.
anti_patterns: Do not capture private, regulated, or confidential pages unless you have permission.
Do not run the skill with an untrusted URL_CHROME_PATH value.
Do not publish captured paywalled or copyrighted content without checking rights.

faq.question: Does this skill require Chrome?
faq.answer: Yes. It launches Chrome or a compatible Chromium browser and controls it through the Chrome DevTools Protocol.

faq.question: Can it capture pages that need login?
faq.answer: Yes, wait mode lets the user prepare the page before capture. Use this only for content you are allowed to save.

faq.question: Where are files saved?
faq.answer: By default, files are saved under a url-to-markdown folder by domain. Users can also provide an output path.

faq.question: Does it send captured content to another service?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the reviewed files. It loads requested web pages and writes Markdown locally.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill launches a browser, opens a local debugging port, runs page extraction code, and can capture authenticated content.

faq.question: Will every page convert cleanly?
faq.answer: No. Complex layouts, interactive widgets, and unusual HTML may need manual cleanup after conversion.
